PARANOIA | 
In a Paranoia adventure, you always know what to expect. Almost al follow the standard ouline we talk about on page 32. Roughly, adventures work like 50 ‘The Troubleshooters receive a mission alen, Itinforms them that The Computer and Alpha Complex once again require their services, land summons them to a briefing where the ‘specific nature of that service may be discuss- ed in private. The Troubleshooters go to the briefing and are told what they have to do. The Troubleshooters go try to do what they have been told todo. They always have plenty Cf exciting adventures, Time passes. The body ‘count rises. When the GM runs out of adven ture or the players run out of clones, the adventure ends, When (i) they complete their mission, the survivors are summoned to a debriefing where they report the results oftheir mission and get praised for thelr loyal service. Itsounds so simple. Somehow it always gets complicated. Each stage in the adventure always seems to get more complex than ithas ‘ny right to be. Terrible things happen over land over again. We Just can’t understand it You'd almost think adventure designers and gamemasters were trying to get the poor Troubleshooters killed. All Paranoia adventures follow a standard scheme, They begin with a mission alert, follow with a briefing, and so on. Players become familiar with the scheme over time. You don't want to let them become too familiar with it; if they learn what to expect, the element of paranoia is lost. Give them the: familiar — then distor it in some appalling and humorous fashion, 
Mission Alerts ‘A mission alert is a Troubleshooter's sum- mons to duty. Mission alerts may be broad: cast over public address systems or com units (and bellowed cheerfully by the GM in his bes foothall-stadium announcer voice) or they arrive as hardcopy via messenger or through ‘4 nearby Computer monitor (as is often the: ‘case when the summons is confidential) The mission alert is our answer to the disconcerting unease we experienced GMs us- ed to have in other roleplaying games when ‘we announced the nature of the evening's adventure, We were always concemed that the players would simply refuse to do what ‘we wanted them to do, because they knew it was dangerous and probably not in their characters’ best interests, anyway. ‘ Phooey. 
Take the stupid ring to the Cracks of Doom yoursel Who cares. I'm going to hang around town and butcher ores." Nobody refuses mission alerts, This is The Computer talking, Everyone always co operates, Cheerfully. And enthusiastically Here are some common features of mission alerts 
‘A Mission Reference Code or Codename (eg, Mission Three-Ex-Ff-Dash-One-Four- Niner, or Mission Code Evinrude): Maybe the characters should memorize the code, Sup- pose some high muck-a-muck or IntSec goon demanded it? How could they prove that they’ee on official business without it? What it the code was incomplete, garbled, or inaccuratet 
‘A Brief Description of the Nature of the Mis- sion (e.g, Find and. apprehend citizen.” “Treason in progress” “Investigate distur- bance in outer space.”): These are frequently incomplete, garbled, inaccurate, obscure, oF purposely misleading, Doesn't mater anyway, You got to doit, whatever itis. Or isn’t. Often ‘omitted for sechrity reasons, or deferred until the Briefing Location of the Briefing and Identity of the Briefing Officer: This is real important; other- wise, you don’t know where to show up for the briefing, Failure to show up fora briefing is treason. What if two digits in the room number were transposed? Or the typist’ fingers were one key to the left on the key- board when the whole message was typed up ‘makes it kinda hard to read. Or if there's no such room? They could request confirma- Aion or assistance from The Computer or other citizens, but there’s no guarantee it won't be ‘equally garbled, misleading, or inaccurate ‘And sometimes The Computer and is servants ‘get huffy if you haven't been paying proper attention to the mission alert, Maybe your Clone will be more attentive 
‘A Stirring Appeal to the Troubleshooters’ Loyalty and Dedication (e.g, "You have been generously permitted another opportunity to serve The Computer and Alpha Complex.” This is generally an opportunity to display the comrect degree of fervent enthusiasm. “Oh, boy. A mission! What fun! And a chance to serve My Friend The Computer! Oh, thank you thank you thank yout" Remember, failure to be happy is treason. 
‘A Misleading or Ironic Understatement of the Mission’s Dangers (eg, “I have the greatest confidence in this mission, Dave-Y. Don't worry. Everything is just fine."); This is a charming feature of The Computer's cheerful, positive style — everything in Alpha Complex 
, 4. The Adventure Stereotype 
\s safe, easy, and fun. As a result, Trouble shooters automatically assume everything is much worse than they are told — and even then they're being optimistic 
Briefings 
Briefings generally take place in a briefing room, However, field briefings are not uncom mon, and can take place anywhere — in cor ridors, zooming autocars, meter-diametertun- nels in the bowels of Alpha Complex, and so 
The briefing officer (or officers) delivers any doiails of the mission cleared for Trouble shooter ears, and answers any questions the Troubleshooters are bold or foolish enough 10 ask Briefing officers are usually accompanied by imposing. guards, guardbots, or automatic security systems, present forthe very purpose ‘of annihilating Troubleshooters who are s ly, uncooperative, or disloyal, oF whose jump Suits aren't neatly pressed, Lots of Trouble: shooters die in briefing rooms. Here the Troubleshooters are given the details oftheir mission, Occasionally some of these details are accurate and reliable: Occa- sionally the briefing officer isn’t teetering on the brink of insanity, Occasionally the brie ing officer isn’t involved in some nefarious plot to sabotage the mission, And occasionally the briefing officer isn’t completely misinformed. Occasionally ‘Often the mission leader is selected at this point, either by the group itself, by the brie: ing officer, or by The Computer, Then the mis sion group may be directed to stop by at PLC Oustitting to pick up equipment a specific lst may be assigned), Then the group may be directed to stop by at R&D to pick up ex- perimental equipment for field testing, The ‘group may also be ordered to seek out various ‘experts, informants, or resource specialists in preparing for the mission. Sometimes these specialists are assigned tothe group in an ad- visory oF leadership role, “Then the group is given their mission odes, These usually include the objectives of the mmission and any special directions or restraints to be observed. The Troubleshooters get one last chance to ask questions, then they are sent ‘out to do the job, 
See Outfitting Alter the Briefing the nest stop is usually Plc. Production, Logistics, and Commissary handles production and distribution of the
